text:
Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
Available online 30 April 2021
0926-6690/© 2021 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. Characterization of multifunctional panels from jute fibers for interior 
wall covering 
Zhi Jin a, Jinyi Li b, Qiangqiang Wang c, Wenbo Su a, Xinfang Duan a, Zhaoqun Tang a,* 
a Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing, 100091, China 
b Suzhou Mowei Natural Fiber Materials Co., Ltd, Jiangsu, 215551, China 
c China Building Material Test and Certification Group Co., Ltd, Beijing, 100024, China   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Jute fibers 
Interior wall covering 
Thermal insulation 
Sound absorption 
Humidity control 
A B S T R A C T   
Jute fibers are an important cash crop in the world with a series of advantages such as high-yield, low-cost and 
easy biodegradation, etc. They also exhibit high potentials for providing hygrothermal and acoustic functions 
due to inherent chemical composition and internal structure. In this paper, multifunctional panels made from 
jute fibers blended with polyester fibers were explored as a new kind of interior wall covering material beneficial 
for both building energy conservation and indoor comfort. The fabricated jute/polyester fiber-based panels (JPs) 
were characterized in terms of morphology, pore structure, surface chemistry and functional performance 
including thermal insulation, sound absorption and humidity control properties. The results showed that thermal 
insulation properties of JPs were comparable for commercial thermal insulators with thermal conductivities of 
0.0372–0.0418 W/(m⋅K), superior to most natural thermal insulators. Sound absorptions mainly occurred in the 
middle to high frequencies, and noise reduction coefficients reached to the commercial level. It was found that 
the thermal and acoustic properties were significantly influenced by the density and thickness of JPs. Moreover, 
JPs exhibited good potential in humidity control with high 24 h desorption ratios of 79.87–96.61 %. The 
maximum 24 h absorption amount was 183.05 g/m2 under high-relative humidity (RH), approximately 3.1 times 
as much as the standard limit value. Meanwhile, the moisture absorption amount was largely dependent on the 
surface chemistry and pore structure of JPs as well as environment RH. In general, JPs could be commercially 
used as a promising interior wall covering material for improving building energy efficiency and indoor envi­
ronmental quality. 1. Introduction 
The production of sustainable materials by usage of natural fibers has 
drawn much attention in building industry due to the threat of global 
warming and increasing demand for green development. Recently, re­
searches have focused on investigating the performance of natural fiber- 
based products with regards to building energy conservation and indoor 
environmental quality improvement. Jerman et al. (2019) showed that 
wood fiberboard, flax fibers, hemp fibers, and jute fib

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
ers presented low 
thermal conductivity, fast water vapor diffusion and convenient hy­
groscopic properties, which made them good candidates for the appli­
cations in interior thermal insulation systems and suitable for 
moderation of indoor air and humidity. Pelletier et al. (2019) examined 
the acoustical absorption property of a novel kind of all natural foams 
produced by a fungi in the phylum class of Basidiomycete. It was 
demonstrated that this material was a promising alternative for 
traditional acoustic absorbers, especially for reducing low to 
mid-frequency range noise (such as road noise). Liuzzi et al. (2020) 
blended straw and olive fibers with a sodium silicate solution as a binder 
respectively to create panels for indoor covering and characterized their 
hygrothermal and sound absorption properties. It was found that the 
straw fiber-based panels exhibited better thermal insulation property 
than olive fiber-based panels as a consequence of more porous structure 
by presence of fibers and greater bulk porosity, and their sound ab­
sorption properties were closely related to the tortuosity due to the way 
fibers were bound together. Jute fibers are one of the most important natural fibers in the world. In some Southeast Asian countries, they are an indispensable cash crop 
which supports the main sources of income for millions of households 
and provides a great deal of employment and livelihood in the relevant 
industrial activities (Shamna et al., 2017). In China, the industrialization 
of jute-based production is preferentially encouraged according to the 
* Corresponding author. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect 
Industrial Crops & Products 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/indcrop 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indcrop.2021.113530 
Received 8 December 2020; Received in revised form 2 April 2021; Accepted 7 April 2021

Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
2
Guidance Catalogue of Industrial Structure Adjustment (2019 edition). As a kind of renewable and fast-growing biomass resource, jute fibers are 
annually available with much shorter growing period than natural 
wood, contributing to their high-yield and low-cost. Furthermore, jute 
fibers possess a series of advantages such as easy biodegradation, low 
gravimetric density, good mechanical performance, antibacterial, high 
absorbency and hygroscopicity, etc (Jahan et al., 2016; Nong et al., 
2019). As the traditional use of jute fibers for packaging has been 
threatened by the advent of cheap synthetic packaging materials and 
bulk handling, more outlets for jute fibers have been concerned in order 
to maintain their commercial status (Maity, 2016). This promotes the 
development of new technologies in the production of jute-based com­
posites, such as nonwovens and fiber reinforcement composites. In 
particular, due to the highest stiffness among all the common natural 
fibers and nonabrasive nature, jute fibers have become an alternative to 
the conv

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
entional glass fibers when used as reinforced fibers (Zakriya 
et al., 2017). Nowadays jute fibers are utilized in a variety of domains 
including packaging, construction, and auto industry (Rezghi Maleki 
et al., 2018; Witayakran et al., 2017). Based on the characteristics in chemical composition and internal 
structure, jute fibers are a kind of promising raw material for the 
exploitation of building materials with hygrothermal and acoustic 
properties. Among the naturally lignocellulosic fibers, jute fibers contain 
high proportions of stiff natural cellulose, approximately 61–72 % by 
mass (Mohanty and Misra, 1995; Gurunathan et al., 2015). Fonseca et al. (2019) affirmed that jute fibers were suitable to be used as raw materials 
for production of cellulose nanofibrils. The high amount of cellulose of 
jute fibers ensures their excellent hygric performance because the 
moisture sorption mainly depends on the presence of free hydroxyl 
groups in the amorphous regions and at crystallite’s surface (Ivanovska 
et al., 2019). In addition, jute fibers are a good insulator of heat and 
sound owing to their inherent porous structure. The thermal conduc­
tivity value of jute fibers (0.036 W/(m K) is lower than many other 
natural fibers, such as wood fibers (0.040 W/(m K), hemp fibers 
(0.042 W/(m K), kapok fibers (0.042 W/(m K), coconut fibers 
(0.058 W/(m K), rattan fibers (0.072 W/(m K), banana fibers 
(0.09 W/(m K) and groundnut shell fibers (0.093 W/(m K) (Cetiner and 
Shea, 2018; Devireddy and Biswas, 2016; Sair et al., 2017; Damfeu et al., 
2016). In the case of jute fiber-reinforced composites, the thermal con­
ductivities are 0.125−0.161 W/(m K (Venkatesan and Kadiresh, 2019), 
which can be defined as an insulator for the thermal conductivity values 
are less than 0.25 W/(m⋅K) (Wang, 1988). For acoustic properties, Yang 
and Yan (2012) showed the best sound absorption property of jute fibers 
and their reinforced composites in comparison to those of flax fibers, 
ramie fibers, synthetic fibers and their reinforced composites. Ahmed 
(2016) illustrated the superior sound absorption properties of jute fabric 
at the frequency range of 100−250 Hz and 4000−6300 Hz which were 
better than that of commercial glass wool. Since the interior wall is a critical region in the building construction 
with an area approximately two and a half times as large as that of the 
floor, the optimization of interior wall covering materials greatly con­
tributes to both high building energy efficiency and good indoor envi­
ronmental quality. However, the traditional interior wall covering 
materials such as latex paint, wall paper and ceramic tile tend to release 
certain amount of formaldehyde, total volatile organic compound, 
heavy metals or radioactivity contaminants, which adversely affects 
indoor environmental quality and imposes a human health risk (Liu and 
Hu, 2011). Furthermore, most of them are lack of relevant functions for 
improving building energy

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
efficiency and indoor environmental quality. Considering the benefits and characteristics of jute fibers mentioned 
above, multifunctional panels made from jute fibers blended with 
polyester fibers were explored as a new kind of interior wall covering 
material in this paper. Due to non-toxic and aldehyde-free properties of 
the raw materials, the obtained jute/polyester fiber-based panels (JPs) 
can be widely used for interior wall covering with a negligible risk of 
toxic and harmful substances emission. The morphology, pore structure, 
surface chemistry and functional performance including thermal insu­
lation, sound absorption and humidity control properties of JPs were 
systematically characterized, and the potential factors affecting the 
functional performance were also revealed. The findings could facilitate 
the practical application of JPs as a new kind of interior wall decorative 
material beneficial for both building energy conservation and indoor 
comfort. 2.

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
Materials and methods 
2.1. Samples 
Jute fibers (JF; glucose: 69.50 ± 0.89 %, xylose: 12.80 ± 0.49 %, 
arabinose: 1.21 ± 0.02 %, acid soluble lignin: 1.89 ± 0.05 %, klason 
lignin: 9.85 ± 0.85 %, ash: 4.80 ± 0.59 % according to the method 
described in S1; degree of crystallinity: 29.48 ± 2.55 % according to the 
method described in S2) and bi-component polyester fibers (BPET, 
polyester core: melting point of 260 ◦C; copolyester sheath: melting 
points of 110–120 ◦C) supplied by Suzhou Mowei Natural Fiber Mate­
rials Co., Ltd. were used to prepare the composite panels. The JF (about 
10−30 mm in length) and BPET (about 10−30 mm in length) were 
blended uniformly in 1:1 dry weight proportion. The blended fibers 
were thoroughly opened by using a pilot card machine and then fed to 
produce a random laid web at various speed (1.2–1.8 m/min) by air 
laying technique. Afterwards, the web was pre-needled with 70 
punches/cm2 to 7–8.5 mm needle penetration depth and further needled 
with 70 punches/cm2 to 4–5.4 mm needle penetration depth. The mass 
per unit area (2000−4000 g/m2) was achieved by adjusting the web- 
formed speed, pre-needled depth and needled depth. After the above 
needle-punched process, thermal bonding process of webs was initiated 
by hot pressing at 200 ◦C with pressure of 1960 K N for 1.5–2.5 min to 
obtain the final panels. The as-prepared JPs were denoted as JPT-X, 
where JP was abbreviated from jute/polyester fiber-based panel, T 
represented the panel thickness (unit: mm), and X was one hundredth of 
the mass per unit area (unit: g/m2). 2.2. Characterization 
2.2.1. Morphology 
The morphologies of raw fibers (JF and BPET) and JPs were char­
acterized using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, 
Carl Zeiss Merlin, Germany) operated at an accelerating voltage at 
20 kV. The samples were sputtered with a gold layer about 10 nm in 
thickness before observation. 2.2.2. Pore structure` 
The pore structure of JF and jute/polyester fiber-based panel fibers 
(JPF) from JP9−20 was determined by nitrogen adsorption/desorption 
isotherms collected at −196 ◦C on an Autosorb-iQ (Quantachrome, USA) 
automatic analyzer. The specific surface area (SBET) and average pore 
width (Dave) were calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET, 
Brunauer et al., 1938) method. The total pore volume (Vtot) was obtained 
from the nitrogen adsorption volume at the maximum relative pressure. The pore size distribution (PSD) was determined by Barrett, Joyner and 
Halenda (BJH, Barrett et al., 1951) method. 2.2.3. Surface chemistry 
The surface chemistry of raw fibers and JPF from JP9−20 was 
investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) performed on 
an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using 
a monochromatic AlKα X-ray (1486.6 eV) source. 2.2.4. Thermal insulation property 
The thermal insulation property of JPs was determined according to 
the methodology established by ISO 8301:, 1991. The specimen with th

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)

e 
size of 300 mm × 300 mm was sandwiched between the hot and the cold

3
plates with a mean temperature (Tmean) of 25 ± 1 ◦C (hot plate: 
35 ± 0.1 ◦C; cold plate: 15 ± 0.1 ◦C). The thermal conductivity was 
calculated by measuring the amount of heat flux and temperature dif­
ference across the specimen thickness as Eq. (2). λ = q × d / △T                                                                              
(2) 
Where, λ is the thermal conductivity, W/(m⋅K); q is the density of heat 
flow-rate, which is the heat through the specimen per unit measured 
area, W/m2; d is the average specimen thickness, m; △T is the difference 
in temperature between the cold and hot surfaces, K. 2.2.5. Sound absorption property 
The sound absorption property of JPs was investigated according to 
ISO 354:, 2003. This method is theoretically based on the diffuse-field 
absorption coefficient rather than normal-incident absorption coeffi­
cient, which is regarded to be more practical for the acoustic perfor­
mance measurement of architectural and environmental applications. The volume of the reverberation room used was 216 m3 and its shape 
was rectangle. The air temperature and relative humidity in the rever­
beration room were 23 ± 2 ◦C and 50 ± 10 %, respectively. The test

text:
Jin et al. Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
4
specimens (about 10 m2) were placed on the floor of the reverberation 
room and installed in accordance with the specification of Annex B B.2 
“Type A mounting” of ISO 354:, 2003. The frequency measured ranged 
from 100−5000 Hz at one-third-octave bands. The values of sound ab­
sorption coefficient (αs) and noise reduction coefficient (NRC) were used 
to represent the sound absorption property of JPs. The αs was evaluated 
based on the reverberation time measured according to the Eq. (3). αs = [55.3V (1/c2T2 - 1/c1T1) -4V (m2 - m1)] / S                                 
(3) 
Where, V is the volume of the empty reverberation room, m3; T1 is the 
reverberation time of the empty reverberation room, s; m1 is the power 
attenuation coefficient of the empty reverberation room, m−1; T2 is the 
reverberation time of the reverberation room after the test specimen has 
been introduced, s; m2 is the power attenuation coefficient of the 
reverberation room after the test specimen has been introduced, m−1; c1 
is the propagation speed of sound in air of the empty reverberation 
room, m/s; c2 is the propagation speed of sound in air of the reverber­
ation room after the test specimen has been introduced, m/s; S is the 
area, covered by the test specimen, m2. NRC value represents the sound absorption ability by providing a 
quantification of the percentage of sound energy absorbed upon hitting 
a particular surface, which is the arithmetic average of the αs values 
determined at frequencies of 250, 500, 1000 and 2000 Hz. Fig.

Jin et al. Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
5
2.2.6. Humidity control property 
The humidity control property of JPs (JP9−20, JP18−27 and 
JP18−40) was measured according to Chinese standard JC/T 
2002–2009. The measurement temperature and relative humidity (RH) 
were controlled using a constant temperature and humidity box. The 
four sides and lower surface of the test specimen (250 mm × 250 mm) 
were wrapped with aluminum foil so that the moisture could only go 
through the upper surface. The test was carried out at 23 ± 0.5 ◦C under 
three different RH conditions of low-RH, mid-RH and high-RH (Table 1). For each condition, the test specimen was firstly balanced in the curing 
RH until its constant mass was considered to be reached when the results 
of two successive weighing operations at an interval of 24 h did not 
differ by more than 0.1 % of the mass of the test specimen. After the 
curing step, the mass was noted m0. Then the specimen was placed in the 
absorption RH of procedure 1 for 24 h and sequentially in the desorption 
RH of procedure 2 for 24 h. The difference between the RH of procedure 
1 and procedure 2 should be within the range of 25 ± 3 %. The mass after 3, 6, 12 and 24 h procedure 1 and procedure 2 was 
respectively recorded. The moisture absorption amount (Wa, g/m2), 
moisture desorption amount (Wd, g/m2) and 24 h desorption rate (Rd, 
%) were calculated as the Eqs. (4)–(6). Wa = (ma - m0) / A                                                                          
(4)  
Wd = (ma - md) / A                                                                          
(5)  
Rd = Wd / Wa × 100 %                                                                     
(6) 
Where, ma is the mass of the test specimen at the end of absorption step, 
g; md is the mass of the test specimen at the end of desorption step, g; m0 
is the mass of the test specimen at the end of curing step, g; A is the 
surface area of the test specimen, m2. 3.

text:
Results and discussion 
3.1. Appearance 
The appearance of JPs is illustrated in Fig. 1. As can be seen, JPs 
present neat and natural look and can be used without surface decora­
tion. In the thickness direction, uniformly distributing fibers indicate 
fine internal structure (Fig. 1e). When applied as indoor decorative wall 
panels, they present high visual quality for creating an elegant atmo­
sphere (Fig. S1).

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
3.2. Morphology 
The morphology of the raw fibers is illustrated in Fig. 2. From Fig. 2a, 
rough surface and irregular-shaped cross section with voids and holes 
are observed in JF, whose diameter varies greatly along the fiber length. In Fig. 2b, a large number of pores and voids are clearly visualized in the 
cross section, demonstrating its intrinsic porous structure. By compari­
son, the BPET exhibits smooth surface and uniform diameter (Fig. 2c), 
with no pores or voids available even at high magnification (Fig. 2d). Fig. 2e-h shows the morphology of JP18−27 and JP18−40. When 
observed perpendicular to the panel’s plane (Fig. 2e-f), the JF and BPET 
fibers well intertwine with each other and both maintain fine fibrous 
morphology. Whereas some surfaces are bonded (as shown in the red 
circles) as a result of the fusion of the low-melting sheath on BPET fibers 
during hot-press procedure. As seen from the cross section (Fig. 2g-h), 
the fibers of JP18−27 is arranged more loosely than those of JP18−40, 
presenting wider interspace. 3.3. Pore structure 
The pore structure of the JF and JPF is further determined by ni­
trogen adsorption/desorption isotherms at −196 ◦C. Both isotherms 
exhibit type IV curve with a long hysteresis, indicative of the dominance 
of mesoporosity (Fig. 3a). The PSD of JPF becomes wider than that of JF 
(Fig. 3b), which suggests the enhancement of porosity for JPF after hot- 
press process. This can also be confirmed by the greater values of SBET, 
Vtot and Dave for JPF (Fig. 3a).

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
3.4. Surface chemistry 
Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of JF, BPET and JPF. The C1s spectrum 
is deconvoluted into four established groups of C–C and C–H 
(284.70–284.82 
eV), 
C–O 
(286.27–286.41 
eV), 
C––O 
(287.87–288.02 eV) and O–C––O (288.75–288.96 eV) (Biniak et al., 
1997; Ishimaru et al., 2006). The XPS surface element analysis and C1s 
functional groups content of JF, BPET and JPF are listed in Table 2. Compared with JF and BPET, JPF shows a decrease in C content and an 
increase in O content with the O/C value increasing by 17.5 % and 31.2 
%, respectively. Moreover, the amount of C––O group of JPF is signifi­
cantly decreased compared to that of JF and simultaneously the amount 
of O–C––O group of JPF is increased. This implies the oxidation reac­
tion of JF during hot-press process, which might be responsible for the 
porosity enhancement of JPF. 3.5. Thermal insulation property 
The thermal conductivity value is a key index to reflect the thermal 
insulation property of products. As shown in Table 3, the thermal con­
ductivities of JPs are decreased with the decrease in bulk density or 
increase in thickness, ranging from 0.0372 to 0.0418 W/(m⋅K), which 
are generally comparable to the commercial glass wool-based thermal 
insulative products with the requirement of “≤0.034−0.050 W/(m⋅K) 
(Tmean: 25 ◦C)” according to GB/T 13350–2017, and the commercial 
Fig. 3. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms at −196 ◦C (a) and the PSD 
of JF and JPF (b). Z.

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
Jin et al. Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
6
rock wool board with the requirement of “≤0.040−0.048 W/(m⋅K) 
(Tmean: 25 ◦C)” according to GB/T 19686–2015. Moreover, they are 
significantly lower than those of other natural thermal insulators. Fig.5 shows the thermal conductivities of natural thermal insulators 
summarized in Table 3 versus bulk density. It is illustrated that the JPs 
exhibit lower thermal conductivities than those of natural thermal in­
sulators with similar bulk density. The excellent thermal insulation 
properties of JPs are mainly due to their interior structure with looser 
fiber arrangement and lager amount of “tortuous path”. The mechanism 
of thermal conduction involves the conduction through fibers and con­
vection through gas molecules trapped within the fiber webs. Thus, a 
lower extent of contiguous fibers diminishes the thermal transport by 
fiber conduction and the increased amount of entrapped air within the 
looser webs leads to lower heat convection as the thermal conductivity 
value of air is significantly lower than that of fiber. Simultaneously, 
more pore walls and “tortuous path” among fibers result in a higher 
probability of elastic collisions between fiber and entrapped air. Since 
the collision between fiber and gas molecules transfers less thermal 
energy than that between gas molecules (Zakriya et al., 2017), the 
amount of energy transfer is reduced and the thermal insulation prop­
erty is consequently improved. 3.6. Sound absorption property 
Fig. 6 shows the αs values of JPs across the entire frequency range. The JPs exhibit good sound absorption properties mainly in the middle 
to high frequencies. For all the samples, the αs values significantly in­
crease with the increase of frequency from 250 to 3150 Hz, and then 
show a slight decrease from 3150 to 5000 Hz. The sound absorption 
properties of JPs are competitive with those of other commercially 
available sound absorption materials. The calculated NRC values lie in 
the standard range of “NRC ≥
0.30−0.50” referring to GB/T

7
25998–2020 for mineral wool decorating and acoustic ceilings. GB/T 
16731–1997 uses the NRC value to classify the sound absorption prop­
erty of absorbent products applied in building constructions into four 
classes: Class I (NRC ≥0.80), Class II (0.8 > N

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
RC > 0.60), Class III 
(0.60 > NRC ≥0.40) and Class Ⅳ(0.40 > NRC ≥0.20). According to this 
classification system, the sound absorption property of JP9−20, 
JP9−25, JP18−27 and JP18−40 can reach to Class Ⅳ, Class Ⅳ, Class III 
and Class II respectively. We can also find that the αs value is dramatically improved by the 
increase in thickness and the increase in bulk density can also raise the αs 
value. For porous materials, when the sound wave is absorbed into the 
bulk, the air particles trapped in pores vibrate and then rub the wall of 
pores, which leads to the dissipation of acoustic energy into heat (Farid 
et al., 2018). As the thickness or bulk density of JPs increases, more 
abundant pore structure is formed and the complexity of sound path 
(tortuosity) where the sound wave and air particles are captured is 
enhanced, contributing to stronger vibration and friction so that more 
acoustic energy is absorbed. Moreover, the JP18−27 presents higher αs 
values than those of JP9−25 over a wide range of frequency larger than 
250 Hz, though its bulk density is lower. This finding is consistent with 
the previous study about the sound absorption characteristics of poly­
urethane and polystyrene foams, which pointed out that using a thicker 
sound absorbent with reasonable density exhibited better performance 
than using a denser thinner one (Sabbagh and Elkhateeb, 2019). Actu­
ally, when the panel is too compact, the sound wave is restricted to 
penetrate into the bulk (Or et al., 2017; Kang et al., 2018). As the fre­
quency is elevated, the sound wave becomes more drastic and more 
difficult to penetrate into a denser panel, resulting in a reduction on the 
sound absorption performance. High αs values ranging from 0.80 to 1.00 
are obtained for JP18−40 over the frequency from 800 to 5000 Hz, 
exceeding those of kenaf fiber sheets with thickness of 25 nm (Lim et al., 
2018). 3.7. Humidity control property 
Table 4 shows the humidity control performance of JP9−20, 
JP18−27 and JP18−40 evaluated by JC/T 2082–2011. All of the testing 
samples present high values of Rd which are larger than the limit value of 
JC/T 2082–2011. In particular, JP18−40 under high-RH condition ex­
hibits admirable humidity control property with the Wa values of 3, 6, 
12, 24 h reaching to 2.8, 2.8, 2.8 and 3.1 times as much as the corre­
sponding limit values according to JC/T 2082–2011. However, moisture 
absorption capacities under low and mid-RH are mostly below the JC/T 
2082–2011 requirements. Fig. 7 illustrates the changes of moisture amount during absorption 
and desorption procedures under mid-RH for different JPs samples 
(Fig. 7a) and under different RH conditions for JP18−40 (Fig. 7b). As 
shown in Fig. 7a, the moisture absorption/desorption amount of JPs 
increases as the density or thickness is increased, which could be

metadata.material_category: Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)
metadata.source_path: data\raw\Natural Plant Fibers (flax, coconut, kenaf, jute)\Jute_Jin.pdf

text:
Jin et al. Industrial Crops & Products 167 (2021) 113530
8
attributed to the increasing amount of hydrophilic groups such as C–O 
and O–C––O groups on the surface of JPF as well as more abundant pore 
structure where the water molecules are stored. The testing samples 
almost achieve equilibrium moisture absorption/desorption amount just 
after 3 h, demonstrating their rapid moisture absorption/desorption 
rate. However, as the absorption rate slows down, the Wa values larger 
than 3 h are obviously below the standard requirement (Table 4), being 
more deteriorated for longer absorption time. This phenomenon is also 
presented in the case of JP18−40 under low-RH as shown in Fig. 7b. While for JP18−40 under high-RH condition, the moisture amount is 
continuously changed during the whole absorption/desorption proced­
ure, which implies that high environment RH could provide a sufficient 
power to make the moisture accessible to the deeper sites of the JPs and 
was appreciably favorable for improving the moisture absorption/ 
desorption rate and capacity. 4. Conclusion 
In this study, a new kind of multifunctional panels for interior wall 
covering made from jute fibers blended with polyester fibers was 
explored. The thermal insulation properties of the fabricated jute/ 
polyester fiber-based panels (JPs) were found to be improved with the 
reduction in density or increase in thickness. The thermal conductivities 
were comparable for commercial glass/rock wool-based thermal insu­
lative products and lower than those of most natural thermal insulators. The excellent thermal insulation properties of JPs are mainly due to 
their interior structure with looser fiber arrangement and lager amount 
of “tortuous path”. The JPs also showed good sound absorption prop­
erties mainly in the middle to high frequencies with NRC values satis­
fying the standard requirements for commercially available sound 
absorption materials applied in building industry. The sound absorption 
property was improved by the increase of thickness or density and 
particularly more dependent on the thickness. Moreover, the JPs 
exhibited good potential in humidity control with high 24 h desorption 
ratios and maximum moisture absorption amount approximately 3.1 
times as much as the standard limit value. The moisture absorption 
amount increases as the density or thickness is increased, closely related 
to the amount of surface hydrophilic groups such as C–O and O–C––O, 
and pore structure of JPs. It was also found that the increase in envi­
ronment RH could significantly improve the moisture absorption/ 
desorption rate and capacity. In general, the JPs were regarded as a 
promising interior wall covering material beneficial for both building 
energy conservation and indoor comfort. CRediT authorship contribution statement 
Zhi Jin: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, 
Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jinyi Li: Resources. Qiangqiang Wang: Resour
